name: Cost-Plus Markup
description: Cost-plus (markup) pricing: add a markup percentage to unit cost to get the selling price, the profit per unit, and the gross margin that markup is equivalent to.

inputs.name: Unit cost
inputs.description: Cost of goods per unit — ideally the landed cost, not just the purchase price.

inputs.name: Markup
inputs.description: Markup as a percentage of cost, e.g. 25 for a 25% markup.

outputs.name: Gross profit
outputs.description: The markup amount: cost times the markup percentage.

outputs.name: Equivalent gross margin
outputs.description: The margin (profit as a percentage of price) that this markup produces.

expression: price = C x (1 + markup/100); profit = C x markup/100; margin_pct = 100 x markup / (100 + markup)
assumptions: Markup is expressed on cost; margin is expressed on price. They are different numbers for the same profit — a 25% markup equals a 20% margin.
Unit cost includes everything counted as cost of goods for the item.
The market will bear the resulting price — cost-plus pricing ignores demand and competition.
limitations: Cost-plus pricing takes no account of what customers will pay or what competitors charge.
A per-unit gross view: it ignores overheads, payment fees, returns, and volume effects.
Confusing markup with margin overstates profitability — apply a 30% markup expecting a 30% margin and you get only 23%.

examples.note: A 25% markup on a 100 cost gives a 125 price and 25 profit — which is a 20% margin on the selling price, not 25%.

sources: Standard cost-plus pricing identity; markup-to-margin conversion

citations.quote: Methods include cost markup pricing. In this model the cost plus a markup percent to attain a desired profit is calculated.
